headline: Spain Wins the World Cup in Extra Time
deck: Ferran Torres settled a bruising final in the 106th minute. Spain’s second title arrived with red cards at MetLife, localized clashes in Buenos Aires and a child’s death during celebrations in Ciudad Rodrigo.
body: Spain left MetLife Stadium as world champion after beating Argentina 1–0 in extra time on 19 July [E1]. Ferran Torres scored the only goal in the 106th minute, turning a locked final with one clean finish [E1][E2]. The strike ended Argentina’s defence of the trophy and delivered Spain the tournament’s last word [E1][E2]. The 2026 World Cup closed under the New Jersey lights with the smallest possible score and a decisive Spanish name [E1].

Torres’s finish came after Argentina had been reduced to ten men at the end of regulation [E1][E3]. The referee showed Enzo Fernández a second yellow card in stoppage time, sending the midfielder off before extra time began [E3]. Argentina therefore played the decisive period with one fewer player, and Spain eventually found the space that settled the match [E1][E3]. Argentina’s attack remained passive and registered no shot, leaving the losing side with a harsher internal question than any argument over officiating [E1][E5].

After the final whistle, Leandro Paredes grabbed Eric García by the throat and shoved Gavi during a melee between the teams [E1]. The referee showed Paredes a straight red card, adding another Argentine dismissal after Enzo’s earlier sending-off [E1][E3]. García and Gavi emerged as the Spanish players at the centre of an altercation that extended the final’s ugliness beyond the last kick [E1]. Further disciplinary consequences had yet to be announced on Monday morning [E1].

Hours later, celebration in Ciudad Rodrigo turned into mourning when a fountain partly collapsed beneath people who had climbed onto it [E4]. A 13-year-old boy was killed, and another minor was injured in the collapse during Spain’s overnight celebrations [E4]. The death brought a human cost far beyond the stadium and the arguments that followed the final [E4]. Local joy over a football victory ended with a family confronting the loss of a child [E4].

Spain woke to its second men’s world title, its first since 2010, and a major-tournament double after winning Euro 2024 [E1][E2]. The national mood was one of release and confirmation for a generation that had now conquered Europe and the world [E1][E2]. Argentina’s grief carried self-criticism because the holders had remained cautious, created almost nothing and reached extra time without a shot [E1][E5]. Lionel Scaloni’s post-match reflection was measured, while the sharper verdict centred on his team’s passivity in the match that mattered most [E5].

Clashes around the Obelisco in Buenos Aires remained localized, with police using water cannon and reports placing the number detained at roughly 12–15 [E1]. No fatalities were reported, and the disorder did not amount to a wider uprising across the capital [E1]. Separately, FIFA was still assessing the “Las Malvinas son argentinas” banner displayed by Argentina’s players after their semifinal victory over England [E6]. No formal disciplinary charge over the banner had been issued by Monday morning [E6].

The final will be remembered first for Torres arriving in the 106th minute and scoring the goal that made Spain champion [E1][E2]. Its disciplinary tail belongs to Enzo Fernández’s second yellow and Paredes’s post-whistle red, neither of which changes how the match was decided [E1][E3]. Its darkest fact lies in Ciudad Rodrigo, where celebration ended beneath a collapsing fountain [E4]. The biggest show on earth folded its stage by dawn: Spain walked home to a party, Argentina to an argument with itself, and one family to a wake [E1][E4][E5].

evidence_box.fragment: Spain battle past 10-man Argentina 1-0 in extra time
Ferran Torres framed as the decisive hero
the second yellow and red card shown to Enzo Fernández

art.caption: The greatest show on earth folds its stage: one fire goes dark as the crowd walks home, some to a party and some to a wake.
